factors.summary: Environmental, social, and governance (ESG) commitments are affecting the company through stock contributions to pension plans.

evidence.text: In the fourth quarter of 2020, we contributed $3 billion of our common stock to our pension fund. In the fourth quarter of 2020, we also began using our common stock in lieu of cash to fund Company contributions to our 401(k) plans for the foreseeable future, which we estimate will conserve approximately $1 billion of cash over the next 12 months.

sentiment.label: neutral
sentiment.rationale: The use of stock contributions to pension plans is a financial decision but does not inherently indicate a positive or negative sentiment towards ESG factors.

factors.summary: Demand trends are predominantly negative, driven by the ongoing impacts of the COVID-19 pandemic, which has caused a significant deceleration in overall demand across various sectors. Additionally, the grounding of the 737 MAX aircraft has contributed to further uncertainty and decline in demand.

evidence.text: The pandemic is having a direct impact on our commercial services business. See Overview to Management’s Discussion and Analysis of Financial Condition and Results of Operations for a discussion of the impacts of COVID-19 on the airline industry environment.

The global outbreak of COVID-19 and the residual impacts of the 737 MAX grounding continue to have significant adverse impacts on our business and are expected to continue to negatively impact revenue, earnings, and operating cash flow in future quarters.

Aircraft values and lease rates are impacted by the number and type of aircraft that are currently out of service. Approximately 7,300 western-built commercial jet aircraft (29.4% of current world fleet) were parked at the end of 2020, including both in-production and out-of-production aircraft types.

The global economy is currently experiencing significant adverse impacts due to the COVID-19 pandemic, including a decline in overall trade in general and in aerospace in particular. These adverse economic impacts have resulted in fewer orders than previously anticipated for our commercial aircraft.

Commercial air traffic has fallen dramatically due to the COVID-19 pandemic. This trend has impacted passenger traffic most severely. Near-term cargo traffic has also fallen, but to a lesser extent as global trade has begun to recover.

sentiment.label: very_negative
sentiment.rationale: The evidence points to significant negative demand trends due to the COVID-19 pandemic and 737 MAX grounding, leading to substantial adverse impacts on revenue and operations.

factors.summary: The order backlog is consistently reported as shrinking across multiple subsections of the SEC filing. The reduction is attributed to factors such as lower customer activity, timing of awards, revenue recognition, and significant events like the 737 MAX grounding, order cancellations, and changes in projected price escalation. Additionally, the COVID-19 pandemic has exacerbated this trend by reducing demand and potentially leading to further cancellations.

evidence.text: Total backlog of $60,847 million at December 31, 2020 was $2,844 million lower than December 31, 2019 due to the timing of awards and revenue recognized.

Revenues decreased by $18,401 million in 2020 compared with 2019 primarily due to lower revenues in our commercial airplanes and commercial services businesses. Revenues for each of our segments have been adversely impacted by COVID-19.
BCC’s gross customer financing and investment portfolio at December 31, 2020 decreased from December 31, 2019, primarily due to $321 million of note payoffs and portfolio run-off.

The decrease in contractual backlog during 2020 was primarily due to a reduction for orders that in our assessment no longer meet the accounting requirements of ASC 606 for inclusion in backlog primarily due to 737 MAX and 777X, deliveries in excess of new orders, aircraft order cancellations and changes in projected price escalation.

sentiment.label: very_negative
sentiment.rationale: The consistent shrinking of the backlog orders due to multiple negative factors, including the pandemic and order cancellations, indicates a very negative sentiment.

factors.summary: Organic revenue growth is decelerating, primarily driven by the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic, production issues with key programs like the 737 MAX and 787, and higher asset impairment expenses.

evidence.text: BDS revenues in 2020 increased by $162 million compared with 2019 reflecting higher revenues from fighter aircraft, Space Launch System, B-52 upgrades, proprietary and MQ-25, partially offset by reduced volume in missile defense.

The COVID-19 pandemic has caused an unprecedented shock to demand for air travel, creating a tremendous challenge for our customers, our business, and the entire commercial aerospace manufacturing and services sector.
Earnings from operations in 2019 decreased by $51 million compared with 2018 primarily due to lower revenues and higher asset impairment expenses.
The 737 program continues to be significantly impacted by the COVID-19 pandemic and its effect on aircraft demand. These impacts have resulted in lower production and delivery rate assumptions.
BCA revenues decreased by $16,093 million in 2020 compared with 2019 due to lower deliveries primarily driven by the impacts of the COVID-19 pandemic, 787 production issues and the 737 MAX grounding.
The COVID-19 pandemic has had a significant impact on our liquidity and overall debt levels. During the year ended December 31, 2020, net cash used by operating activities was $18.4 billion.

sentiment.label: very_negative
sentiment.rationale: Decelerating organic growth driven by the pandemic, production issues, and higher expenses suggests a very negative sentiment.

factors.summary: Unit volumes are declining across multiple product lines, driven by factors such as the 737 MAX grounding, reduced demand due to the COVID-19 pandemic, and operational disruptions. The decrease in deliveries and higher rates of aircraft retirement further contribute to the negative volume trends.

evidence.text: Deliveries of units for new-build production aircraft, including remanufactures and modifications were as follows: Years ended December 31, 2020 2019 2018 F/A-18 Models 20 23 17 F-15 Models 4 11 10 C-17 Globemaster III 1 CH-47 Chinook (New) 27 13 13 CH-47 Chinook (Renewed) 3 22 17 AH-64 Apache (New) 19 37 AH-64 Apache (Remanufactured) 52 74 23 KC-46A Tanker 14 28 P-8 Models 15 18 16 C-40A 2 Total 154 229 96

Revenues decreased by $18,401 million in 2020 compared with 2019 primarily due to lower revenues in our commercial airplanes and commercial services businesses. Revenues for each of our segments have been adversely impacted by COVID-19.

Of these parked aircraft, a larger portion are expected to be retired compared to the pre-COVID-19 period, which directly impacts the Company in terms of number of new aircraft deliveries and financing opportunities.

While we resumed deliveries of 737 MAX aircraft in December 2020, the 737 MAX grounding is still in effect in certain non-U.S. jurisdictions. The 737 MAX grounding will continue to have a significant impact on future revenues until deliveries ramp up, and COVID-19 will continue to have a significant impact on future revenues until the commercial airline industry recovers.

Commercial air traffic has fallen dramatically due to the COVID-19 pandemic. This trend has impacted passenger traffic most severely. Near-term cargo traffic has also fallen, but to a lesser extent as global trade has begun to recover.

sentiment.label: very_negative
sentiment.rationale: Declining unit volumes across product lines due to the pandemic and operational disruptions point to a very negative sentiment.

factors.summary: The end market momentum is predominantly negative, particularly in the commercial aerospace sector, which is facing severe challenges due to the COVID-19 pandemic and the grounding of the 737 MAX. While the government services and defense markets are relatively stable, they are not immune to the broader economic impacts.

evidence.text: The demand outlook for our government services business has remained stable in 2020. Government services market segments are growing on pace with related fleets, but vary based on the utilization and age of the aircraft.

The COVID-19 pandemic has caused an unprecedented shock to demand for air travel, creating a tremendous challenge for our customers, our business, and the entire commercial aerospace manufacturing and services sector.

The commercial airplane market and the airline industry both remain extremely competitive. While the impacts and responses have varied globally, the reduction of demand and disruption in production has adversely impacted most manufacturers in the commercial airplane industry.

Commercial air traffic has fallen dramatically due to the COVID-19 pandemic. This trend has impacted passenger traffic most severely. Near-term cargo traffic has also fallen, but to a lesser extent as global trade has begun to recover.

sentiment.label: very_negative
sentiment.rationale: Negative momentum in the commercial aerospace sector with stable but uncertain government markets indicates a very negative sentiment.

factors.summary: The defense budget environment is mixed, with stable current funding but uncertainty about future appropriations. While some areas show growth, there are risks related to potential budget pressures and shutdowns.

evidence.text: The enacted FY21 appropriations included funding for Boeing’s major programs, such as the F/A-18 Super Hornet, F-15EX, CH-47 Chinook, AH-64 Apache, V-22 Osprey, KC-46A Tanker, P-8 Poseidon and Space Launch System. However, there continues to be uncertainty with respect to future program-level appropriations for the U.S. DoD and other government agencies, including NASA.
evidence.subsection: United States Government Defense Environment Overview

evidence.text: At BDS, we continue to see a healthy market with solid demand for our major platforms and programs both domestically and internationally.

Levels of U.S. defense spending are very difficult to predict and may be impacted by numerous factors... A lapse in appropriations for government departments or agencies would result in a full or partial government shutdown, which could impact the Company’s operations.

sentiment.label: neutral
sentiment.rationale: Mixed signals about defense budget stability and future appropriations suggest a neutral sentiment.

factors.summary: The margin trajectory is negative, with multiple factors contributing to compressing margins. Lower revenues, increased costs, production issues, and the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic are all cited as pressures on margins. While there is evidence of improved margins in some areas, such as BCC’s operating margins increasing, the overall trend indicates a decline in margins.

evidence.text: Gross and operating margins are compressing.
The effective income tax rate decreased to 17.5% in 2020 from 71.8% in 2019, but GAAP earnings from operations were negative in 2020 compared to positive in 2019.
BCC’s operating margins increased to 24% in 2020 compared with 11% in 2019.

The program method of accounting allocates tooling and other non-recurring and production costs over the accounting quantity for each program. Because of the higher unit production costs experienced at the beginning of a new program and substantial investment required for initial tooling and other non-recurring costs, new commercial aircraft programs, typically have lower initial margins than established programs and a higher risk for reach-forward loss.

Cost of sales as a percentage of Revenues increased in 2020 compared to 2019 primarily due to the reach-forward loss on the 777X program, impacts of the 737 MAX grounding and the COVID-19 pandemic, as well as severance costs. [...] The decrease in 737 deliveries and earnings charges related to the 737 MAX grounding.
evidence.subsection: Total Costs and Expenses (“Cost of Sales”)

evidence.text: The COVID-19 pandemic has had a significant impact on our liquidity and overall debt levels. During the year ended December 31, 2020, net cash used by operating activities was $18.4 billion.

sentiment.label: very_negative
sentiment.rationale: Compressed margins due to lower revenues, increased costs, and pandemic impacts indicate a very negative sentiment.

factors.summary: The near-term risks are predominantly negative, with multiple factors contributing to challenges. These include the ongoing impacts of the COVID-19 pandemic on customer demand, supply chains, and liquidity, as well as the continued disruptions from the 737 MAX grounding. Additionally, there are concerns about potential further production cuts, the financial viability of airlines, and geopolitical trade tensions affecting the global economy.

evidence.text: The level of profitability on the 777X program will be subject to a number of factors. These factors include continued market uncertainty, the impacts of COVID-19 on our production system as well as on our supply chain and customers, subsequent production rate reductions for both 777X and other commercial programs, and potential risks associated with the testing program and the timing of 777X certification.

The current environment is also affecting the financial viability of some airlines. We currently expect it will take approximately three years for worldwide travel to return to 2019 levels and a few years beyond that for the industry to return to long-term trend growth of approximately 5%.
The impacts of the COVID-19 pandemic and 737 MAX grounding are expected to continue to have a significant negative impact on our operating cash flows during 2021.
In the event that we are unable to resume aircraft deliveries in non-U.S. jurisdictions consistent with our assumptions, our expectation of delivery timing could be impacted.

The global economy is currently experiencing significant adverse impacts due to the COVID-19 pandemic, including a decline in overall trade in general and in aerospace in particular. [...] The U.S. and China imposed an escalating series of tariffs on each other’s imports. [...] We are closely monitoring the current impact and potential future economic consequences of COVID-19 to the global economy, the aerospace sector, and our Company.
The COVID-19 pandemic has had a significant impact on our liquidity and overall debt levels. During the year ended December 31, 2020, net cash used by operating activities was $18.4 billion.

sentiment.label: very_negative
sentiment.rationale: The factor highlights significant negative risks including the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic, 737 MAX grounding, production cuts, airline viability, and geopolitical tensions.

factors.summary: The contract backlog is primarily affected by reductions due to the 737 MAX grounding, order cancellations, and production delays on the 777X program. While some sections indicate a decrease, others show stability or mention specific program-related issues without overall change.

evidence.text: Total backlog of $60,847 million at December 31, 2020 decreased by 10% from $22,902 million at December 31, 2019, primarily due to a reduction for commercial orders that, in our assessment, no longer meet the accounting requirements of ASC 606 for inclusion in backlog.

The decrease in contractual backlog during 2020 was primarily due to a reduction for orders that in our assessment no longer meet the accounting requirements of ASC 606 for inclusion in backlog primarily due to 737 MAX and 777X, deliveries in excess of new orders, aircraft order cancellations and changes in projected price escalation.
We now anticipate that the first 777X delivery will occur in late 2023. We also recorded a $6.5 billion reach-forward loss on the 777X program in the fourth quarter of 2020.

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: While some areas are stable, reductions due to 737 MAX and 777X issues suggest ongoing challenges.

factors.summary: The competitive dynamics in the industry are intensifying, driven by numerous domestic and international competitors, improvements in efficiency by competitors, and government subsidies that are putting pressure on market share and pricing. While there is no explicit mention of new entrants or aggressive rivals in the financial position section, the overall trend indicates increased competition.

evidence.text: Industry Competitiveness Aviation services is a competitive market with many domestic and international competitors. This market environment has resulted in intense pressures on pricing, and we expect these pressures to continue or intensify in the coming years.

The industry continues to adjust to the unprecedented COVID-19 shock and subsequent economic impact, government restrictions, and new regulations. The commercial airplane market and the airline industry both remain extremely competitive. [...] Competitors continue to make improvements in efficiency, which may result in funding product development, gaining market share and improving earnings. This market environment has resulted in intense pressures on pricing and other competitive factors, and we expect these pressures to continue or intensify in the coming years.

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: Increased competition from domestic and international firms, efficiency improvements, and government subsidies are pressuring market share and pricing.

factors.summary: International sales are significant and face both opportunities and challenges. The pipeline is expanding, especially in Asia, Europe, and the Middle East, while also being impacted by reduced demand, travel restrictions, and geopolitical tensions. Risks such as tariffs and trade relations could affect future performance.

evidence.text: BDS expects that it will continue to have a wide range of opportunities across Asia, Europe and the Middle East given the diverse regional threats. At the end of 2020, 31.8% of BDS backlog was attributable to non-U.S. customers.
evidence.subsection: United States Government Defense Environment Overview

evidence.text: The recovery remains slow and uneven as travel restrictions and varying regional travel protocols continue to impact air travel. Generally, we expect domestic travel to recover faster than international travel.

In 2020, non-U.S. customers... accounted for approximately 37% of our revenues. We expect that non-U.S. sales will continue to account for a significant portion of our revenues for the foreseeable future... Impacts from these or future potential tariffs, or deterioration in trade relations between the U.S. and one or more other countries, could have a material adverse impact on our revenues, operating earnings, and/or cash flows.

Approximately 80% of Commercial Airplanes’ total backlog, in dollar terms, is with non-U.S. airlines. [...] The U.S. and China imposed an escalating series of tariffs on each other’s imports. [...] We are closely monitoring the current impact and potential future economic consequences of COVID-19 to the global economy, the aerospace sector, and our Company.
evidence.subsection: We derive a significant portion of our revenues from non-U.S. sales and are subject to the risks of doing business in other countries.

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: Despite opportunities, risks like reduced demand, travel restrictions, and geopolitical tensions pose significant challenges.

factors.summary: Program execution is facing significant challenges, including delays and cost overruns across multiple major programs such as the 777X, KC-46A Tanker, and 737 MAX. These issues are exacerbated by production inefficiencies, design modifications, and the impacts of the COVID-19 pandemic on both operations and market demand.

evidence.text: The KC-46A Tanker reach-forward loss of $1,320 million reflects $551 million of costs associated with the agreement signed in April 2020 with the U.S. Air Force to develop and integrate a new Remote Vision System, and the remaining costs reflect production inefficiencies including impacts of COVID-19 disruption.
The loss in 2020 primarily reflects a reach-forward loss recorded in the fourth quarter of $6,493 million on the 777X program.

The revised schedule and the reach-forward loss reflect a number of factors, including an updated assessment of global certification requirements informed by continued discussions with regulators and resulting in a management decision to make modifications to the aircraft’s design, an updated assessment of COVID-19 impacts on market demand, and discussions with our customers with respect to aircraft delivery timing.

The 2020 loss reflects the reach-forward loss on 777X of $6,493 million, lower deliveries and lower program margins resulting from the COVID-19 pandemic and 787 production issues and associated rework, $2,567 million of abnormal production costs related to 737 MAX, $623 million of severance cost, $498 million of 737 MAX customer considerations, $336 million related to 737NG frame fitting component repair costs and $270 million of abnormal production costs in the first half of 2020 from the temporary suspension of operations in response to COVID-19, partially offset by lower research and development spending.

sentiment.label: very_negative
sentiment.rationale: Multiple major programs face delays, cost overruns, and inefficiencies due to various challenges, indicating significant execution issues.

factors.summary: The company is implementing cost reduction programs and actions, which are delivering some savings but may require further efforts. While these actions include production rate adjustments, workforce reductions, and lower research and development spending, the savings might be offset by factors such as increased production costs and potential risks from future public health crises.

evidence.text: Cost reduction programs are delivering savings but may require further actions.
We expect to incur approximately $5.0 billion of abnormal production costs that are being expensed as incurred.
Lower research and development spending. [...] The decrease in research and development expense in 2020 was primarily due to lower spending at BCA and at Boeing NeXt on product development.
We may face similar risks in connection with any future public health crises, including resurgences in the spread of COVID-19.

sentiment.label: neutral
sentiment.rationale: While cost reduction efforts are ongoing, there are offsetting factors like increased production costs and future health risks, making the sentiment unclear.

factors.summary: The revenue mix is shifting towards lower-margin segments due to multiple factors, including decreased demand in commercial airplanes and services, the impact of the 737 MAX grounding, and the broader effects of the COVID-19 pandemic. While there are isolated mentions of gains from re-leasing assets, the overall trend indicates a shift towards lower margins.

evidence.text: Revenues decreased by $18,401 million in 2020 compared with 2019 primarily due to lower revenues in our commercial airplanes and commercial services businesses. Revenues for each of our segments have been adversely impacted by COVID-19.
BCC’s revenues in 2020 increased by $17 million compared with 2019 primarily due to gains on re-lease of assets.
We are producing at abnormally low production rates in 2020 and 2021, and we expect to incur approximately $5.0 billion of abnormal production costs that are being expensed as incurred.

The 2020 loss reflects the reach-forward loss on 777X of $6,493 million, lower deliveries and lower program margins resulting from the COVID-19 pandemic and 787 production issues and associated rework, $2,567 million of abnormal production costs related to 737 MAX, $623 million of severance cost, $498 million of 737 MAX customer considerations, $336 million related to 737NG frame fitting component repair costs and $270 million of abnormal production costs in the first half of 2020 from the temporary suspension of operations in response to COVID-19, partially offset by lower research and development spending.

Near-term cargo traffic has also fallen, but to a lesser extent as global trade has begun to recover. Most airlines have significantly reduced their capacity, and many could implement further reductions in the near future.

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: Shift towards lower-margin segments due to decreased demand and pandemic effects, indicating a negative trend.

factors.summary: Labor costs are a significant factor with mixed implications. While severance and restructuring costs are creating margin pressure, other factors such as production adjustments and cost reduction activities are helping to manage these costs. Additionally, there are concerns about potential increases due to COVID-19 related operational disruptions.

evidence.text: During 2020, we recorded severance costs for approximately 26,000 employees, of which approximately 18,000 have left the Company as of December 31, 2020, and the remainder are expected to leave in 2021.

The 2020 loss reflects [...] $623 million of severance cost, $498 million of 737 MAX customer considerations, $336 million related to 737NG frame fitting component repair costs and $270 million of abnormal production costs in the first half of 2020 from the temporary suspension of operations in response to COVID-19, partially offset by lower research and development spending.

We expect to incur approximately $5.0 billion of abnormal production costs that are being expensed as incurred. The slowdown in the planned production rate ramp-up increased expected abnormal costs however this increase was offset by adjustments to the determination of the normal production level due to COVID-19 impacts on customer demand, as well as cost reduction activities, including significant reductions in employment levels.
We continue to experience additional operating costs due to social distancing requirements and other factors related to COVID-19 restrictions.

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: Severance costs and operational disruptions from COVID-19 are pressuring margins.

factors.summary: Productivity is showing mixed performance. While operational excellence initiatives and production rate improvements are enhancing efficiency, operational disruptions and challenges related to the COVID-19 pandemic are negatively impacting productivity.

evidence.text: These activities are not intended to constrain our capacity, but to enable the Company to emerge stronger and be more resilient when the market recovers.
The year-over-year increase in non-cash items also reflects higher inventory write-downs and higher allowances for expected credit losses in 2020.

Deferred production, unamortized tooling and other non-recurring costs are expected to be fully recovered when all units in the accounting quantity are delivered as the expected unit cost for later deliveries is below the estimated average cost as learning curve and other improvements are realized.

We continue to experience additional operating costs due to social distancing requirements and other factors related to COVID-19 restrictions.

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: Productivity is mixed with improvements but significant disruptions from COVID-19.

factors.summary: The supply chain is facing significant disruptions due to the ongoing impacts of the COVID-19 pandemic, including operational issues, liquidity pressures on suppliers, and financing programs affecting payables. Additionally, production uncertainties and fewer orders, particularly related to the 737 MAX, further strain supply chain health.

evidence.text: Our suppliers are also experiencing liquidity pressures and disruptions to their operations as a result of COVID-19.

Payables to suppliers who elected to participate in supply chain financing programs declined by $1.9 billion for the year ended December 31, 2020, and increased by $2.6 billion and $0.6 billion for the same period in 2019 and 2018.
These impacts have resulted in lower production and delivery rate assumptions. We currently expect to gradually increase the production rate to 31 per month by early 2022.

The global economy is currently experiencing significant adverse impacts due to the COVID-19 pandemic, including a decline in overall trade in general and in aerospace in particular. [...] We are experiencing fewer new 737 MAX orders than we were receiving prior to the grounding.

Many of our suppliers also were required to suspend operations during the second quarter of 2020, and they may experience additional disruptions in 2021. Any such disruptions could have severe adverse impacts on our production costs, delivery schedule and/or ability to meet customer commitments.

sentiment.label: very_negative
sentiment.rationale: Significant disruptions from COVID-19 and 737 MAX issues severely impact supply chain.

factors.summary: The company is facing challenges with inventory levels, which are at risk due to lower demand, potential excess, and issues such as production halts, delivery delays, and supply chain problems. The inventory has increased, particularly due to undelivered aircraft and the grounding of the 737 MAX, with approximately 425 airplanes in inventory as of December 31, 2020. There are concerns about the impact on future revenues, earnings, and cash flows if deliveries are not timely made.

evidence.text: If we are unable to make timely deliveries of the large number of aircraft in inventory as of December 31, 2020, future revenues, earnings, and cash flows will be adversely impacted.

Cash used by investing activities during 2020, 2019, and 2018 was $18.4 billion, $1.5 billion, and $4.6 billion. The increase in cash outflows in 2020 compared to 2019 is primarily due to $17.4 billion of higher net contributions to investments.

We have approximately 425 airplanes in inventory as of December 31, 2020. A number of customers have requested to defer deliveries or to cancel orders for 737 MAX aircraft, and we are remarketing and/or delaying deliveries of certain aircraft included within inventory.

We had not previously experienced a complete suspension of our operations at these production sites. While we have resumed operations at all of our production sites we cannot predict whether or where further production disruptions could be required or what the ongoing impact of COVID-19-related operating restrictions will be.

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: Excess inventory and delivery delays pose risks to future cash flows.

factors.summary: Management has provided cautious forward guidance, primarily due to the ongoing impacts of the COVID-19 pandemic and the 737 MAX grounding. The company anticipates a slow recovery, with travel returning to pre-pandemic levels within three years and several years beyond that to reach long-term growth trends. The 737 MAX production is expected to gradually increase to 31 units per month by early 2022, contingent on market demand. The risks associated with the pandemic and the grounding continue to pose significant challenges, potentially affecting future operating cash flows and the need for additional financing.

evidence.text: We currently expect it will take approximately three years for worldwide travel to return to 2019 levels and a few years beyond that for the industry to return to long-term trend growth of approximately 5%
The impacts of the COVID-19 pandemic and 737 MAX grounding are expected to continue to have a significant negative impact on our operating cash flows during 2021

We currently expect to gradually increase the production rate to 31 per month by early 2022. We currently assume that we will implement further gradual production rate increases in subsequent periods based on market demand

We expect negative operating cash flows in future quarters until deliveries begin to return to historical levels, and if the pace and scope of the recovery are worse than we currently contemplate, we may need to obtain additional financing in order to fund our operations and obligations

sentiment.rationale: Cautious outlook with significant risks from pandemic and 737 MAX grounding.

factors.summary: Management confidence is mixed, with cautious optimism regarding long-term recovery and industry fundamentals, but also concerns about the 737 MAX program, the ongoing impacts of COVID-19, and market competition.

evidence.text: The long-term outlook for the industry remains positive due to the fundamental drivers of air travel demand: economic growth, increasing propensity to travel due to increased trade, globalization, and improved airline services driven by liberalization of air traffic rights between countries.

We are focused on improving our products and services and continuing our business transformation efforts, which enhances our ability to compete and positions us for market recovery. We are also focused on taking actions to ensure that Boeing is not harmed by unfair subsidization of competitors.

sentiment.label: neutral

factors.summary: The company's long-term strategy appears to be mixed. While there is a focus on core businesses and future programs, execution is hindered by significant risks, challenges, and uncertainties, particularly related to the 737 MAX grounding and the ongoing impact of the COVID-19 pandemic.

evidence.text: Our strategy is centered on successful execution in healthy core businesses – Commercial Airplanes (BCA), Defense, Space & Security (BDS), and Global Services (BGS) – supplemented and supported by Boeing Capital (BCC).
Our debt balances have increased significantly since 2019, and we are continuing to actively manage our liquidity.

We currently expect to gradually increase the production rate to 31 per month by early 2022. We currently assume that we will implement further gradual production rate increases in subsequent periods based on market demand.
The magnitude and duration of the global COVID-19 pandemic is uncertain and continues to adversely affect our business and operating and financial results.

factors.summary: R&D investment is decreasing across the company, particularly in key areas like BCA and Boeing NeXt, due to cost reduction measures and lower spending on product development.

evidence.text: Through our portfolio and investment prioritization, we reduced research and development and capital expenditures during 2020 by $1.3 billion from the prior year.
Research and development expense decreased by $743 million in 2020 compared with 2019 primarily due to lower spending at BCA and at Boeing NeXt on product development.

factors.summary: Key sensitivities include geopolitical trade tensions, government defense budgets, and the duration of the COVID-19 pandemic.

evidence.text: The pace of the commercial market recovery will be heavily dependent on COVID-19 infection rates, progress on testing, government travel restrictions, and timing and availability of a vaccine.
A number of factors could cause us to incur increased borrowing costs and to have greater difficulty accessing public and private markets for debt.

factors.summary: The company is facing challenges with new product launches, particularly delays in the 777X program, which has resulted in significant losses and operational issues. Additionally, market conditions and economic uncertainties, exacerbated by the COVID-19 pandemic, are impacting the success and timelines of new product introductions.

The 2020 loss reflects the reach-forward loss on 777X of $6,493 million, lower deliveries and lower program margins resulting from the COVID-19 pandemic and 787 production issues and associated rework, $2,567 million of abnormal production costs related to 737 MAX, $623 million of severance cost, $498 million of 737 MAX customer considerations, $336 million related to 737NG frame fitting component repair costs and $270 million of abnormal production costs in the first half of 2020 from the temporary suspension of operations in response to COVID-19, partially offset by lower research and development spending.
We may face similar risks in connection with any future public health crises, including resurgences in the spread of COVID-19.

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: Significant delays and losses in the 777X program, along with pandemic impacts, hinder new product success.

factors.summary: Shareholder returns have been negatively impacted by the company's decision to suspend dividends and halt share repurchases, which were previously used to return value to shareholders. However, the business environment suggests a positive long-term outlook driven by fundamental factors such as economic growth and increased travel propensity, which could potentially support shareholder returns in the future.

evidence.text: The long-term outlook for the industry remains positive due to the fundamental drivers of air travel demand: economic growth, increasing propensity to travel due to increased trade, globalization, and improved airline services driven by liberalization of air traffic rights between countries.

In March 2020, the Company announced that our dividend will be suspended until further notice. During the twelve months ended December 31, 2020, we did not repurchase any shares through our open market share repurchase program.

We expect negative operating cash flows in future quarters until deliveries begin to return to historical levels, and if the pace and scope of the recovery are worse than we currently contemplate, we may need to obtain additional financing in order to fund our operations and obligations.

sentiment.label: neutral

factors.summary: The company's capital expenditure outlook is mixed. While cost-saving measures have led to a reduction in CapEx, the expected consistency with 2020 levels in 2021 suggests cautious confidence. However, uncertainties related to production delays, the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on cash flows, and reduced spending on product development indicate a cautious approach to future investments.

evidence.text: Through our portfolio and investment prioritization, we reduced research and development and capital expenditures during 2020 by $1.3 billion from the prior year.
We expect capital expenditures in 2021 to be relatively consistent with 2020.

We are working with our customers to minimize the impact to their operations from grounded and undelivered aircraft. We continue to reassess the liability for estimated potential concessions and other considerations to customers on a quarterly basis.
Research and development expense decreased by $743 million in 2020 compared with 2019 primarily due to lower spending at BCA and at Boeing NeXt on product development.

We expect negative operating cash flows in future quarters until deliveries begin to return to historical levels, and if the pace and scope of the recovery are worse than we currently contemplate, we may need to obtain additional financing in order to fund our operations and obligations.

sentiment.label: neutral
sentiment.rationale: Reduced CapEx but cautious approach due to production delays and pandemic uncertainties.

factors.summary: Free cash flow is negatively impacted across multiple areas, including lower revenues, increased costs, significant operating cash outflows, production suspensions, and inventory management challenges. The company anticipates continued negative operating cash flows in future quarters.

evidence.text: The effective income tax rate decreased to 17.5% in 2020 from 71.8% in 2019, but GAAP earnings from operations were negative in 2020 compared to positive in 2019.
Net cash used by operating activities was $18.4 billion during 2020, compared with net cash used by operating activities of $2.4 billion during 2019.

In addition, we reduced the number of aircraft included in the accounting quantity by 400 units in the first quarter of 2020 as a result of reductions to planned production rates due to COVID-19 driven market uncertainties.

We expect negative operating cash flows in future quarters until deliveries begin to return to historical levels, and if the pace and scope of the recovery are worse than we currently contemplate, we may need to obtain additional financing in order to fund our operations and obligations.

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: Negative cash flows and challenges in cash management due to lower revenues and increased costs.

factors.summary: Input costs appear to be stable based on the information from the Financial Position subsection, but there is a risk of disruptions due to supply chain instability as mentioned in the Risks Related to COVID-19 subsection.

evidence.text: BCC enters into certain transactions with Boeing, reflected in Unallocated items, eliminations and other, in the form of intercompany guarantees and other subsidies that mitigate the effects of certain credit quality or asset impairment issues on the BCC segment.

Many of our suppliers also were required to suspend operations during the second quarter of 2020, and they may experience additional disruptions in 2021. Any such disruptions could have severe adverse impacts on our production costs, delivery schedule and/or ability to meet customer commitments.

sentiment.label: neutral

factors.summary: The company's pricing power appears to be under pressure. While there is no direct evidence from the Off-Balance Sheet Arrangements subsection, other sections indicate weakening pricing power due to aircraft valuation declines, failed price increases, and customer financial difficulties.

evidence.text: Aircraft valuations could decline if significant numbers of additional aircraft, particularly types with relatively few operators, are placed out of service.

The decrease in 737 deliveries and earnings charges related to the 737 MAX grounding. [...] BCA revenues decreased by $16,093 million in 2020 compared with 2019 due to lower deliveries primarily driven by the impacts of the COVID-19 pandemic, 787 production issues and the 737 MAX grounding. This was partially offset by lower charges related to estimated potential concessions and other considerations to 737 MAX customers of $498 million in 2020 compared with $8,259 million in 2019.

evidence.text: Our customers may also lack sufficient liquidity to purchase new aircraft due to impacts from the pandemic. We are also observing a significant increase in the number of requests for payment deferrals, contract modifications, lease restructurings and similar actions, and these trends may lead to additional charges, impairments and other adverse financial impacts in our business over time.

sentiment.label: very_negative
sentiment.rationale: The company's pricing power is under significant pressure due to declining aircraft valuations, failed price increases, and customer financial difficulties.

factors.summary: Customer concentration is a significant risk for Boeing, as its portfolio is heavily concentrated across specific aircraft types like the 717 and 747-8, and approximately 80% of its Commercial Airplanes' backlog is with non-U.S. airlines, exposing it to international market risks and competition.

evidence.text: BCC’s portfolio is also concentrated by varying degrees across Boeing aircraft product types, most notably 717 and 747-8 aircraft.

Approximately 80% of Commercial Airplanes’ total backlog, in dollar terms, is with non-U.S. airlines. We face aggressive international competitors who are intent on increasing their market share. They offer competitive products and have access to most of the same customers and suppliers.

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: High customer concentration across specific aircraft types and non-U.S. airlines poses a risk, making the company vulnerable to market fluctuations and international competition.

factors.summary: The company's ability to maintain differentiation is challenged by increasing competition, particularly from international competitors offering similar products and services. While the company has competitive advantages, these are under pressure, and the business environment is characterized by intense pricing and market competition.

evidence.text: BCC’s customer financing and investment portfolio at December 31, 2020 decreased from December 31, 2019, primarily due to $321 million of note payoffs and portfolio run-off.

We face aggressive international competitors who are intent on increasing their market share. They offer competitive products and have access to most of the same customers and suppliers. [...] This market environment has resulted in intense pressures on pricing and other competitive factors, and we expect these pressures to continue or intensify in the coming years.

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: Intense competition from international players is challenging the company's ability to maintain differentiation, leading to pressures on pricing and market share.

factors.summary: The company's market share appears to be under pressure, with evidence suggesting potential losses, particularly in the single-aisle aircraft segment due to the 737 MAX grounding and increased competition. evidence.text: BCC’s gross customer financing and investment portfolio at December 31, 2020 decreased from December 31, 2019, primarily due to $321 million of note payoffs and portfolio run-off.

evidence.text: The grounding of the 737 MAX and the associated suspension of 737 MAX deliveries significantly reduced our market share with respect to deliveries of single aisle aircraft in 2019 and 2020 and may provide competitors with an opportunity to obtain more orders and increase market share.

sentiment.label: negative

factors.summary: Quality and safety issues, particularly related to the 737 MAX, are significantly impacting the company. These issues include compensation payments, grounding of aircraft, and reduced revenues.

evidence.text: Compensation payments to 737 MAX customers totaled $2.2 billion during 2020 and $1.2 billion during 2019.

In 2019, following two fatal 737 MAX accidents, the Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) and non-U.S. civil aviation authorities issued orders suspending commercial operations of 737 MAX aircraft. Deliveries of the 737 MAX were suspended following these orders.

The 737 MAX grounding will continue to have a significant impact on future revenues until deliveries ramp up, and COVID-19 will continue to have a significant impact on future revenues until the commercial airline industry recovers.

factors.summary: Capacity utilization is likely decreasing across the company due to multiple factors, including reduced demand, aircraft retirements, production suspensions, and operational constraints related to the COVID-19 pandemic. The evidence points to a sustained period of low utilization, with specific challenges such as the grounding of the 737 MAX and disruptions in production and supply chains.

evidence.text: Aircraft values and lease rates are impacted by the number and type of aircraft that are currently out of service. We temporarily suspended 737 MAX production beginning in January 2020. We resumed early stages of 737 MAX production in May 2020 and continued to produce at low rates through the end of 2020.

Cost of sales decreased by $8,250 million in 2020 compared with 2019, primarily due to lower revenue in 2020, partially offset by higher charges in 2020 related to the 777X program, COVID-19 impacts, KC-46A Tanker program, abnormal production costs at BCA and severance costs.
